subject: ap-psychology
title: Motivation and Social Psychology — Concept Application
stimulus: Rayce is exploring the idea of using online videos to sell his line of custom-made skateboards. While his friend is making a video of him performing tricks at a local skate park, a large crowd forms to watch. Each time Rayce successfully completes a trick, the crowd loudly applauds and yells, showing their approval.

parts.prompt: Explain how Vestibular sense relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that this sense influences Rayce's skateboarding by providing  a sense of balance (e.g., equilibrium, information about position of the head in relation  to gravity). 

OR 

The response must indicate that disruption of this sense leads to dizziness.

required_elements: Rayce's vestibular sense allows him to maintain balance as he's performing tricks.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce's vestibular sense allows him to maintain balance as he's performing tricks.

required_elements: Rayce becomes dizzy doing the stunts because his vestibular sense is out of whack.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce becomes dizzy doing the stunts because his vestibular sense is out of whack.

wrong_examples: Rayce's inner ear helps him do well as he skateboards.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that the vestibular sense provides a sense of balance or equilibrium (information about the position of the head relative to gravity), and must connect this to Rayce's skateboarding. Alternatively, the response may describe what happens when this sense is disrupted — dizziness. Naming the inner ear without explaining the function (balance/equilibrium) does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Rayce's vestibular sense allows him to maintain balance as he's performing tricks.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the vestibular sense's function (balance/equilibrium) and applies it to Rayce performing skateboard tricks.

sample_responses.response: Rayce becomes dizzy doing the stunts because his vestibular sense is disrupted.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable alternate application — disruption of the vestibular sense causes dizziness, which can affect skateboarding performance.

sample_responses.response: Rayce's inner ear helps him do well as he skateboards.
sample_responses.commentary: Naming the inner ear without explaining its specific function (balance, equilibrium, head position relative to gravity) is insufficient.

parts.prompt: Explain how Social facilitation relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that Rayce will perform his tricks better if there is an  audience present. 

OR 

The response must indicate Rayce will perform less well at a certain trick if he is not very  good at that trick if an audience is present.

required_elements: After the crowd gathers, his jumps are higher, and his turns are tighter because of the crowd's presence.
alternatives.correct_example: After the crowd gathers, his jumps are higher, and his turns are tighter because of the crowd's presence.

required_elements: Because he is doing a fairly new trick the presence of a crowd impedes his performance.
alternatives.correct_example: Because he is doing a fairly new trick the presence of a crowd impedes his performance.

wrong_examples: Rayce is very social, so he enjoys performing in front of the crowd.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Social facilitation is the phenomenon whereby the presence of an audience enhances performance on well-learned tasks but impairs performance on novel or difficult tasks. The response must state that Rayce performs better because of the crowd (for well-practiced tricks) OR that the crowd's presence impairs a trick he is not yet skilled at. Simply saying Rayce 'enjoys' performing for the crowd describes social motivation, not social facilitation.

sample_responses.response: After the crowd gathers, his jumps are higher and his turns are tighter because of the crowd's presence.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies social facilitation correctly — well-practiced tricks are enhanced by audience presence.

sample_responses.response: Because Rayce is attempting a fairly new trick, the presence of the crowd impedes his performance.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies the impairing side of social facilitation — for tasks not yet mastered, an audience worsens performance.

sample_responses.response: Rayce is very social, so he enjoys performing in front of the crowd.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes social enjoyment or personality, not social facilitation. Social facilitation is a specific performance effect of audience presence on task execution, not about enjoying crowds.

parts.prompt: Explain how Incentive theory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Rayce is motivated to perform because he is receiving  some external reward (e.g., money, praise, applause, cheering, approval).

required_elements: Rayce  rides his skateboard for the crowd because he wants the applause from the crowd.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce  rides his skateboard for the crowd because he wants the applause from the crowd.

wrong_examples: Rayce likes skateboarding because he finds it fun.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Incentive theory holds that behavior is motivated by external rewards (positive incentives). The response must indicate that Rayce is motivated to perform by some external reward — specifically the applause, cheering, approval, or potential business gains from the crowd. Saying Rayce skateboards because he finds it fun describes intrinsic motivation, not incentive theory.

sample_responses.response: Rayce rides his skateboard for the crowd because he wants the applause from the crowd.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the external reward (crowd applause) as the incentive motivating Rayce's performance.

sample_responses.response: Rayce is motivated to perform tricks because the crowd's cheering and approval serves as an external reward that pulls him to keep performing.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the external incentive (crowd approval/cheering) driving Rayce's behavior.

sample_responses.response: Rayce likes skateboarding because he finds it fun.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes intrinsic motivation (personal enjoyment), not incentive theory, which requires an external reward as the motivator.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Divergent thinking relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate Rayce uses or considers many possible approaches to the  campaign. 

OR 

The response must refer to divergent thinking as "thinking outside the box," innovation,  or creativity.

required_elements: Rayce used multiple approaches to his campaign with both a video and trying the skateboard free for an hour.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce used multiple approaches to his campaign with both a video and trying the skateboard free for an hour.

required_elements: Rayce came up with innovative ideas like the bright graphics.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce came up with innovative ideas like the bright graphics.

required_elements: Rayce thinks creatively about how he can sell more skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce thinks creatively about how he can sell more skateboards.

wrong_examples: Rayce used bright graphics and cool music.
Rayce comes up with the one best way to sell a skateboard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Divergent thinking is the ability to generate many different, creative solutions to a problem — thinking 'outside the box.' The response must indicate that Rayce uses multiple, creative approaches to his marketing campaign, OR describe his thinking as innovative or creative. Simply listing what Rayce did (used graphics and music) without labeling it as creative/divergent is insufficient. Describing convergent thinking (finding the one best solution) is the opposite and does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Rayce used multiple approaches to his campaign — both making a video and allowing people to try the skateboard free for an hour — showing he generated many creative solutions.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies multiple approaches and explicitly connects them to divergent (creative, multiple-solution) thinking.

sample_responses.response: Rayce thinks creatively about how he can sell more skateboards by coming up with the idea of offering a free trial.
sample_responses.commentary: Labels Rayce's marketing strategy as creative thinking, which is a hallmark of divergent thinking.

sample_responses.response: Rayce used bright graphics and cool music in his video.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes what Rayce did but does not identify or apply the concept of divergent thinking — no connection to generating multiple or creative solutions.

sample_responses.response: Rayce comes up with the one best way to sell a skateboard.
sample_responses.commentary: Finding the single best solution describes convergent thinking, not divergent thinking.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Peripheral route to persuasion relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate the campaign used attention-grabbing elements.

required_elements: Rayce uses graphics and music in his video to persuade people to buy his skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce uses graphics and music in his video to persuade people to buy his skateboards.

required_elements: Customers are persuaded to buy the boards by the cool soundtrack in the video.
alternatives.correct_example: Customers are persuaded to buy the boards by the cool soundtrack in the video.

wrong_examples: Rayce informs people about a study that was conducted that showed that his skateboards are the best.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The peripheral route to persuasion involves changing attitudes through superficial, attention-grabbing cues (visuals, music, celebrity appeal, emotions) rather than through logical argument or evidence. The response must indicate that Rayce's campaign uses peripheral cues — the bright graphics and fast-tempo music — to persuade customers, rather than presenting evidence or logical arguments about the skateboard's quality. Describing the central route (logical evidence, expert testimony) does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Rayce uses bright graphics and fast-tempo music in his video to persuade people to buy his skateboards — these attention-grabbing elements work through the peripheral route rather than logical argument.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the peripheral cues (graphics, music) and correctly labels them as the peripheral route (emotional/superficial persuasion, not evidence-based).

sample_responses.response: Customers are persuaded to buy the boards by the cool soundtrack and exciting visuals in the video.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the peripheral cues (music, visuals) as the means of persuasion — the peripheral route relies on these surface-level attention-grabbers.

sample_responses.response: Rayce informs people about a study that was conducted that showed that his skateboards perform better than the competition.
sample_responses.commentary: Presenting data or logical arguments describes the central route to persuasion, not the peripheral route.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Reciprocity norm relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that because potential customers are getting something  from Rayce (e.g., one-hour free trial, a sticker, cool hat), they feel obliged to return the  favor by making a purchase or promoting the product.

required_elements: Because Rayce allowed them to try the skateboard for free, customers returned the favor by buying a skateboard.
alternatives.correct_example: Because Rayce allowed them to try the skateboard for free, customers returned the favor by buying a skateboard.

wrong_examples: Rayce was nice and gave customers one free hour with the skateboard, so they reciprocated.
People saw Rayce do cool tricks in the video and wanted to do cool tricks like him.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain the reciprocity norm by indicating that customers received something from Rayce (the free one-hour trial) and therefore felt obligated to return the favor (by buying a skateboard or promoting the brand). Simply using the word 'reciprocated' without explaining the obligation to return a specific favor does not earn the point. Seeing Rayce do tricks and wanting to imitate describes modeling or social influence, not reciprocity norm.

sample_responses.response: Because Rayce allowed them to try the skateboard for free, customers felt obligated to return the favor by buying a skateboard.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the reciprocity norm: receiving the free trial creates a felt obligation to give back by purchasing.

sample_responses.response: Rayce was nice and gave customers one free hour with the skateboard, so they reciprocated.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the term 'reciprocated' without explaining what was given back or why they felt obligated — too vague to earn the point.

sample_responses.response: People saw Rayce do cool tricks in the video and wanted to do cool tricks like him.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes social learning/imitation (modeling), not the reciprocity norm. Reciprocity requires that something was given first, creating an obligation to return the favor.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Mere-exposure effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate repeated experience to any element of the ad campaign  leads to increased liking or purchasing.

required_elements: The more often that people see the video the more likely they are to buy the skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: The more often that people see the video the more likely they are to buy the skateboards.

wrong_examples: Customers used the skateboard for an hour, so they liked it more.
As soon as customers are exposed to the skateboard, they love it and think it is so cool.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The mere-exposure effect is the finding that repeated exposure to a stimulus increases liking for it. The response must indicate that repeated exposure to any element of Rayce's campaign (the video, the brand, the skateboard itself) leads to increased liking or likelihood of purchase. A single exposure that causes immediate liking does not describe the mere-exposure effect — the key is repetition over time leading to increased liking. Using the skateboard for one hour (a single trial) does not demonstrate the mere-exposure effect.

sample_responses.response: The more often people see Rayce's video online, the more likely they are to develop a liking for his skateboards and eventually purchase one.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the mere-exposure effect — repeated exposure to the video increases liking over time.

sample_responses.response: Customers used the skateboard for an hour, so they liked it more.
sample_responses.commentary: One trial use (trying the board for an hour) is a single exposure, not repeated exposure. The mere-exposure effect requires multiple exposures over time, not a single experience.

sample_responses.response: As soon as customers are exposed to the skateboard, they love it.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes immediate liking from a single exposure, not the gradual increase in liking from repeated exposures that defines the mere-exposure effect.
